text: ‘society’. Van Gennep sure enough refused Durkheim’s societal determinism. Yet it is difficult to accept how this alone would have justified a complete rupture between Durkheim and his students and van Gennep. In his argument, Belier buys into the false premise (in itself a legacy of Durkheim) that we are left with a choice to study either the individual or the collective level. As Marcel Mauss himself would come to realize (most visibly so in his works on the notion of the person and on the techniques of the body), Durkheim’s collectivist position was itself untenable. Rather than effectively sociologizing ‘Western individualism’ (as Belier suggests), Durkheim quite simply transposed the ‘closed self’ to the level of society, replacing one ‘ideology’ with another. As Victor Turner (1988: 84) would later put it, ‘Extreme individualism only understands a part of man. Extreme collectivism only understands man as a part’. Belier’s explanation of why Durkheim, Mauss and their followers could nonchalantly dismiss van Gennep’s work is quite simply not plausible. They had too many interests in common for that to be the case. How are we then to understand this disregard of van Gennep? In the 1960 Introduction written by Solon T. Kimball to the English translation of Rites de Passage it was suggested that the general failure to include van Gennep’s contribution had to do with the fact that later authors on religion (among them Durkheim) were interested in ‘quite other subjects’ than the ones treated in van Gennep’s work (Kimball, 1960: xi). This, however, is quite plainly wrong. Van Gennep’s book not only contains a masterly comparative analysis of existing ethnographic data that could not be bypassed; his analytical framework alone would have been be relevant to anyone studying religion and ritual – and this is still so today. So why, more generally, was van Gennep kept away from French academic circles? The beginning of an answer, as Zumwalt (1982) saw so clearly, is to be found in van Gennep’s opposition to Durkheim. It is with the substance of that critique that we must now engage. Van Gennep’s Critique of Durkheim: Where, When and What? Van Gennep started to develop his critique of Durkheim from early on. His first most explicitly worded critical discussion can be found in his 1906 book Mythes et Légendes, and it actually goes straight to the heart of Durkheim’s position in a surprisingly frank and direct way. And this was the problem with van Gennep’s attitude: what he thought he wrote. He was never circumspect in his critique of Durkheim. The discussion of Durkheim is part of the very long Introduction to this his second book. Before 1906, Durkheim and Mauss had written several essays on religion referring to the Australian material. So had van Gennep. It is noteworthy that whereas van Gennep refers to Mauss and Durkheim throughout (and from the very first paragraph of the Preface), as he engages his critique, he targets Durkheim Liminality and the Modern 50 only. Maybe van Gennep simply did not want to offend his former mentor and friend; but it might also indicate that he knew quite well that the problems he wished to confront belonged primarily to Durkheim and much less so to Mauss. Van Gennep’s book starts by singling out the most important works published in English, French and German on the Australian material, in a sort of user-friendly annotated bibliography. 3 It then starts to discuss the merits and shortcomings of existing approaches, and on page XXIV it is Durkheim’s turn. The critique starts off from a seemingly trivial issue, but one which was then the object of endless debate: whether it was possible to find out which descent system was the ‘original one’. Among the Arunta (one of the Australian clan societies that was also to take centre stage also in EFRL), males run most of the institutions, but at the same time they display clear signs of matrilineal descent. Durkheim had taken this as an indication and indirect proof that matrilineal descent was the original mode of social organization, and that over time it had been replaced by patrilineal descent. Van Gennep argues back that in many Australian societies parallel systems can and do co-exist in time and space, and that it is really rather risky to speculate about origins in the absence of further empirical evidence. At a more general level, van Gennep exposes Durkheim’s underlying evolutionist stance that lurks behind his allegedly sociological and ‘timeless’ explanatory apparatus. This has been pointed out again and again since, but it has rarely, if ever, been fully recognized that van Gennep was the first to raise this critique explicitly, and in direct connection to Durkheim’s empirical writings on religion. Durkheim wanted to cut off historical explanations from sociological science, but the moment he sets out to theorize himself, he plunges into evolutionist speculation. Van Gennep therefore also questions the adopted analytical procedure by which Durkheim (and in this he was not alone, of course) posits the Arunta at a certain level or stage of ‘development’, allowing him an analytical short-cut to the question of ‘origins’. Whenever Durkheim recognizes a change, over time, or between one group and another (in kinship affiliations, for example), he systematically prevents any real account of such a transformation, relegating it simply to the ‘general needs of society’ (ibid.: XXV). As van Gennep points out again and again, Durkheim leaves it utterly unexplained what those ‘needs’ really amount to and how they emerge. There is no grounding epistemology to tell us what such a ‘society’ is to be able to ‘have’ such needs. In fact, says van Gennep, what Durkheim operates is a peculiar kind of ‘métaphysique sociologique’ (1906: XXIV). Durkheim posits a metaphysical abstraction at the core of his argument, and then he artificially ‘animates’ it (ibid.: XXV), granting ‘society’ explanatory powers without ever accounting for its existence.
